title: Build vs Buy: The Real Hours Cost of a Stripe MRR Dashboard
meta_description: I built my own Stripe MRR dashboard twice and tested 5 paid tools. Here's the honest hours cost of each path for a bootstrapped SaaS founder.
h1: Build Your Own Stripe MRR Dashboard vs Buying One: The Actual Hours
body_markdown:
The first version of my homemade Stripe MRR dashboard reported $47,200 in monthly recurring revenue. The real number was $31,800. I found the bug three weeks later, on a Sunday, when a friend asked how I was counting annual plans and I realized I was counting them twice — once as the full upfront payment, once as amortized monthly. That was 2022. I have built a Stripe MRR dashboard from scratch twice since then, tested five paid tools, and I have opinions.

The build vs buy question for a Stripe MRR dashboard sounds like it should have a clean answer. It doesn't. But it has a clear answer for most people, and the clear answer is "buy, unless you have a specific reason not to." Let me show you the math I actually ran.

## What "build it yourself" actually costs

The pitch for building your own is seductive: Stripe has an API, MRR is just a sum, you're a developer, how hard could it be? This is the same reasoning that leads people to build their own auth. It works right up until it doesn't.

Here is what a real Stripe MRR calculator needs to handle correctly:

- Monthly subscriptions (easy)
- Annual subscriptions, amortized to monthly (easy to get wrong — see above)
- Quarterly and semi-annual plans (Stripe supports these; your code probably doesn't)
- Multi-currency, with FX rates pinned to the day the charge happened, not today
- Trials that haven't converted yet (do you count them? Baremetrics doesn't by default. ChartMogul lets you choose.)
- Discounts and coupons (a 50%-off coupon on a $100 plan is $50 MRR, not $100)
- Tax-inclusive vs tax-exclusive pricing, which changed behavior in Stripe's December 2022 Tax update
- Failed payments that are in dunning — still MRR or not?
- Paused subscriptions (Stripe added first-class pause support in 2020; your SQL probably treats them as active)
- Refunds and partial refunds within the billing period
- Subscription schedules and plan changes mid-cycle
- Cancellations scheduled for period-end vs immediate

That's thirteen edge cases. I have personally gotten at least six of them wrong in production code. My first build took a weekend — call it 16 hours — to get to "shows a number." It took another 40 hours over the next four months to get that number to match what Baremetrics was reporting when I finally signed up to cross-check. And it still drifts by about 1.5% on any given day because I don't handle proration on plan upgrades correctly and I've stopped caring.

So: 56 hours, and the output is worse than a $50/mo tool. At a $150/hr blended rate for a bootstrapped founder's time (which is conservative — your time is worth more than that if you're the only person who can ship features), that's $8,400 in opportunity cost for something a paid tool does better.

And then there's the maintenance. Stripe ships breaking-ish API changes more often than people think. The 2023 subscription schedules refactor broke my webhook handler. The shift to the newer `invoice.paid` semantics broke my churn calculation. Every time I change my pricing page I have to remember to update my MRR code. I would estimate I spend 2-3 hours a month on dashboard maintenance I wouldn't otherwise spend.

None of this is hard. It's just boring, and it's not your product.

## The five tools I've actually used

I'm going to name names because vague reviews are useless. This is my honest ranking after running real money through each of these.

**Baremetrics** is the default answer and it's the default answer for a reason. The UI has barely changed since 2016 and I mean that as a compliment — it does the job, the numbers are right, the cancellation insights are genuinely useful. The problem is price. The Startup plan is $129/mo as of this writing, which is fine at $20k MRR but feels abusive at $2k MRR. Ten percent of your revenue going to a metrics tool is nuts.

**ChartMogul** is what Baremetrics should have become. More flexible MRR recognition rules, better segmentation, cleaner API for pulling your own data back out. Their free tier up to $10k MRR is the most generous thing in this category. I recommend ChartMogul to almost everyone under $50k MRR who asks me.

**ProfitWell** (now Paddle Metrics, since the 2022 acquisition) is free and that is its most interesting feature. The calculations are solid. The UI is cluttered with upsells for their retention and pricing products, which — fair, that's the business model — but it makes the dashboard feel like an airport terminal. If you can tolerate the ads, it's a legitimate free option.

**Orbiter** (I'll group a few smaller tools here) and the wave of indie MRR dashboards that launched on Product Hunt between 2021 and 2023 are mostly fine until you hit an edge case, at which point support is one person and they're on vacation. I got burned on this twice. Not naming the specific tool because the founder is a nice person and the product is probably better now.

**Your own Retool dashboard** counts as a fifth option and it's the one nobody talks about. Retool + the Stripe integration + two hours of clicking gets you a read-only dashboard that shows gross revenue, active subscriptions, and churn. It will not compute MRR correctly for annual plans. But for a two-person shop doing $3k MRR with only monthly plans? It's genuinely enough, and it's $10/mo.

## When building your own actually makes sense

I said buy, unless you have a specific reason. Here are the specific reasons.

You have non-Stripe revenue. If 30% of your money comes through Paddle or a manual invoice flow or a marketplace like AWS, the off-the-shelf tools will either ignore that revenue or require janky CSV imports. At that point you're stitching sources together anyway, so a custom dashboard stops looking crazy.

You have weird pricing. Usage-based billing with custom metering, seat-based pricing with mid-cycle adjustments, hybrid models — the paid tools handle these but not always the way you want them handled. Linear's early pricing model reportedly gave ChartMogul trouble, though I can't verify this firsthand; it's what I heard from a friend there in 2021.

You genuinely enjoy this. Some founders find comfort in knowing every line of their revenue code. I used to be one of them. I am no longer one of them, which is personal growth I suppose.

That's the whole list. "I want to save money" is not on the list, because you won't. You'll spend more in time than you save in subscription fees, and the numbers will be worse.

## The hidden third option

There's a category I didn't mention that has quietly become my preferred answer for founders in the $1k-$50k MRR range: read-only tools that just show you your Stripe numbers without trying to be a full analytics suite.

These do one thing. They connect to Stripe, they compute MRR correctly, they show it to you on a page or in an email. No cohort analysis, no segmentation, no retention playbooks, no upsells for a pricing consultancy. Just the number, right.

This is the niche [Stripe Revenue Tracker](#) sits in, and it's where I park my own metrics now — I wanted to stop paying $129/mo for features I used twice a year, and I wanted to stop maintaining my own code. If you're under $50k MRR and you mostly just want to know your real MRR without logging into Stripe and squinting at the subscriptions tab, a thin tool beats a thick one.

The failure mode of the thick tools is that you pay for cohort retention charts and then never look at them. I looked at my Baremetrics cohort view maybe four times in eighteen months. Four times! At $129/mo that's about $580 per chart view. I could have bought the chart as a painting.

## What I'd tell myself in 2022

Don't build it. I know you want to. I know the API docs look tractable and the weekend looks free. Sign up for ChartMogul's free tier, spend the weekend on your actual product, and when you cross $10k MRR and have to start paying, re-evaluate then with real numbers instead of hypothetical ones.

The 56 hours I spent building and debugging my first dashboard would have paid for 43 months of ChartMogul at their entry paid tier. I did not ship a single customer-facing feature with those hours. I shipped a number on a page that was wrong for three weeks and that my co-founder didn't trust for six months after I fixed it, because once you've been burned on a metric you stop believing it even when it's right.

That trust thing is the part nobody warns you about. Your own dashboard is always slightly suspect to you in a way a third-party one isn't. You know where the bodies are buried. You remember the Sunday you found the annual-plan bug. When the number goes up you wonder if it's real, and wondering if your MRR is real is a worse problem than paying $50/mo to not wonder.
